overview.BriefSummary: The Maroochy Water Breach campaign targeted key personnel in a water treatment facility using spear-phishing attachments, aiming to manipulate the wastewater control system.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Shared DB or directory, Web server services such as emails and databases, Shadow IT, Poor implementation of access controls, Authentication issues
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: External/Removable media, Web, Account takeover, Vulnerability exploit, Phishing, Impersonations/Spoofing, Business Email Compromise(BEC)

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2016-4171 [CWE-22]: Vulnerability in glibc in Linux allows a specially crafted string to trigger a heap-based buffer overflow
stage.description: The attackers sent a spear-phishing email with a malicious attachment to key personnel in the water treatment facility, exploiting a vulnerability in glibc to execute arbitrary code once opened.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2021-31955 [CWE-94]: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting') in Microsoft SharePoint Server
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 253 - Name: Remote Code Inclusion
stage.description: After gaining initial access, the attackers exploited a cross-site scripting vulnerability in Microsoft SharePoint Server to upload malware onto the target's system, enabling them to execute arbitrary commands.

Scenario.summary: The Maroochy Water Breach campaign successfully gained unauthorized access to a water treatment facility's systems through spear-phishing and subsequent exploitation of vulnerabilities, ultimately causing significant disruption and environmental damage.
